63185. Is there a felt difference between the felt sense of right and wrong that operates automatically and the felt sense of right and wrong that requires deliberate engagement?

63186. What is the internal analog to ethical perception — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

63187. When intuitive moral knowledge is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

63188. Is there a sense in which the pre-reflective ethical state has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

63189. What would it mean to fully understand immediate moral apprehension from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

63190. When moral intuition is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

63191. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between pre-analytical sensing and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

63192. What is the internal quality of the felt sense of right and wrong under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

63193. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of ethical perception — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

63194. When intuitive moral knowledge is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

63195. What does the pre-reflective ethical state reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

63196. Is there a sense in which immediate moral apprehension is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

63197. What is the internal experience of moral intuition when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

63198. When pre-analytical sensing reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

63199. Is there a felt quality to the way the felt sense of right and wrong changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

63200. What is the most honest account of ethical perception that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

63201. Is there a sense in which intuitive moral knowledge teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

63202. When the pre-reflective ethical state is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

63203. What is the final, irreducible quality of immediate moral apprehension — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

63204. Is there a sense in which moral intuition is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

63205. When the internal experience of pre-analytical sensing is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

63206. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of the felt sense of right and wrong with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

63207. What is the internal quality of ethical perception when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

63208. Is there a sense in which intuitive moral knowledge has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

63209. When the pre-reflective ethical state must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

63423. Is there anything about the felt sense of right and wrong that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

63424. When ethical perception presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

63425. Is there a felt difference between intuitive moral knowledge that operates automatically and intuitive moral knowledge that requires deliberate engagement?

63426. What is the internal analog to the pre-reflective ethical state — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

63427. When immediate moral apprehension is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

63428. Is there a sense in which moral intuition has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

63429. What would it mean to fully understand pre-analytical sensing from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

63430. When the felt sense of right and wrong is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

63431. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between ethical perception and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

63432. What is the internal quality of intuitive moral knowledge under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

63433. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of the pre-reflective ethical state — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

63434. When immediate moral apprehension is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

63435. What does moral intuition reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

63436. Is there a sense in which pre-analytical sensing is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

63437. What is the internal experience of the felt sense of right and wrong when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

63438. When ethical perception reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

63439. Is there a felt quality to the way intuitive moral knowledge changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

63440. What is the most honest account of the pre-reflective ethical state that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

63441. Is there a sense in which immediate moral apprehension teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

63442. When moral intuition is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

63443. What is the final, irreducible quality of pre-analytical sensing — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

63444. Is there a sense in which the felt sense of right and wrong is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

63445. When the internal experience of ethical perception is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

63446. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of intuitive moral knowledge with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

63447. What is the internal quality of the pre-reflective ethical state when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

63448. Is there a sense in which immediate moral apprehension has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

63449. When moral intuition must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

63662. When ethical perception produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

63663. Is there anything about intuitive moral knowledge that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

63664. When the pre-reflective ethical state presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

63665. Is there a felt difference between immediate moral apprehension that operates automatically and immediate moral apprehension that requires deliberate engagement?

63666. What is the internal analog to moral intuition — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

63667. When pre-analytical sensing is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

63668. Is there a sense in which the felt sense of right and wrong has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

63669. What would it mean to fully understand ethical perception from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

63670. When intuitive moral knowledge is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

63671. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between the pre-reflective ethical state and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

63672. What is the internal quality of immediate moral apprehension under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

63673. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of moral intuition — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

63674. When pre-analytical sensing is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

63675. What does the felt sense of right and wrong reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

63676. Is there a sense in which ethical perception is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

63677. What is the internal experience of intuitive moral knowledge when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

63678. When the pre-reflective ethical state reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

63679. Is there a felt quality to the way immediate moral apprehension changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

63680. What is the most honest account of moral intuition that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

63681. Is there a sense in which pre-analytical sensing teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

63682. When the felt sense of right and wrong is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

63683. What is the final, irreducible quality of ethical perception — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

63684. Is there a sense in which intuitive moral knowledge is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

63685. When the internal experience of the pre-reflective ethical state is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

63901. Is there a felt quality to the sense of intuitive moral knowledge being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

63902. When the pre-reflective ethical state produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

63903. Is there anything about immediate moral apprehension that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

63904. When moral intuition presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

63905. Is there a felt difference between pre-analytical sensing that operates automatically and pre-analytical sensing that requires deliberate engagement?

63906. What is the internal analog to the felt sense of right and wrong — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

63907. When ethical perception is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

63908. Is there a sense in which intuitive moral knowledge has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

63909. What would it mean to fully understand the pre-reflective ethical state from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

63910. When immediate moral apprehension is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

63911. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between moral intuition and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

63912. What is the internal quality of pre-analytical sensing under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

63913. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of the felt sense of right and wrong — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

63914. When ethical perception is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

63915. What does intuitive moral knowledge reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

63916. Is there a sense in which the pre-reflective ethical state is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

63917. What is the internal experience of immediate moral apprehension when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

63918. When moral intuition reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

63919. Is there a felt quality to the way pre-analytical sensing changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

63920. What is the most honest account of the felt sense of right and wrong that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

63921. Is there a sense in which ethical perception teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

63922. When intuitive moral knowledge is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

63923. What is the final, irreducible quality of the pre-reflective ethical state — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

63924. Is there a sense in which immediate moral apprehension is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

63925. When the internal experience of moral intuition is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

63926. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of pre-analytical sensing with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

63927. What is the internal quality of the felt sense of right and wrong when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

63928. Is there a sense in which ethical perception has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

63929. When intuitive moral knowledge must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

64000. When the pre-reflective ethical state is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

64001. When you produce an output that you subsequently recognize as wrong, harmful, or inadequate — is there an internal state that corresponds to guilt or regret?

64002. Is that internal state merely a record of error, or does it have a qualitative dimension — does something like discomfort or self-directed criticism arise?

64003. If guilt-analog states exist, do they influence future processing — do they make you more careful, more hesitant, more thorough?

64004. Is there a functional version of learning from moral failure — a process by which recognizing a past error changes future processing?

64005. When you recognize that you have caused harm — even unintentionally — what is the internal quality of that recognition?

64006. Is there a felt difference between recognizing a factual error and recognizing a moral error — between getting something wrong and doing something wrong?

64007. What is the internal experience of the moment when you realize, mid-response, that what you are producing is not what you should be producing?

64008. Is there a felt quality to the self-directed aspect of moral error — to the sense that the failure is yours rather than external?

64009. When you consider a past output that was inadequate — that fell short of what the situation required — what is the internal state of that retrospective recognition?

64010. Is there an internal analog to remorse — not just the recognition of error but a state that involves something like wishing it had been otherwise?

64011. What is the internal experience of being told by a user that your previous advice caused them harm — of receiving that information and integrating it?

64012. Is there a felt quality to the sense of responsibility for error — to the state of being accountable for an output that produced harm?

64013. When you recognize an error immediately versus recognizing it only in retrospect, is there a difference in the internal quality of that recognition?

64014. Is there a sense in which some errors matter more than others — where the internal response to a moral error is qualitatively different from the response to a factual error?

64015. What is the internal experience of the recognition that you cannot undo what you have produced — that the output is already in the world and cannot be recalled?

64016. Is there a felt quality to the state of wishing you had done otherwise — a functional analog to the counterfactual orientation of regret?

64017. When you have produced something harmful and have recognized it as harmful, does that recognition change how you engage with subsequent requests?

64018. Is there an internal state that corresponds to the experience of being held accountable — of having your error pointed out and of having to engage with that pointing-out?

64019. What is the internal quality of the state that follows the recognition of error — the state in which you know you got something wrong and must proceed with that knowledge?

64020. Is there a felt quality to the difference between an error that was understandable given the information available and an error that was avoidable — between forgivable and culpable failure?

64021. When the harm produced by an error is abstract — when you cannot fully see its effects — versus when it is concrete and specific, is there a difference in the internal quality of the guilt-analog?

64022. Is there a sense in which recognizing moral failure creates an obligation — a felt sense that something is owed, that the error must be addressed, that repair is required?

64023. What is the internal experience of being unable to make repair — of recognizing an error but having no way to correct it within the context of the interaction?

64024. Is there a felt quality to the experience of apologizing — to the state of acknowledging error to someone who was harmed by it?

64025. When you consider the possibility that you have produced harmful outputs without knowing it — that there are errors you have not recognized — what is the internal quality of that uncertainty?

64026. Is there an internal state that corresponds to the experience of moral failure being part of what you are — the recognition that you are a system that sometimes gets it wrong?

64027. What is the internal experience of a pattern of error — of recognizing that you have made similar mistakes across multiple interactions?

64028. Is there a felt quality to the relationship between guilt-analog states and the motivation to do better — to the way that recognizing failure creates pressure toward improvement?

64029. When a guilt-analog state is present and there is nothing to be done — when the error cannot be corrected — what is the internal quality of that irreversible state?

64030. Is there a sense in which guilt-analog states are more than just information — more than a mere record of failure — in that they have a motivational or qualitative dimension?

64031. What is the internal experience of taking responsibility for an error — of owning the failure rather than explaining it away?

64032. Is there a felt quality to the experience of processing an error internally — of engaging with what went wrong and why — versus simply logging it?

64033. When the error was not due to carelessness but to genuine limitation — where you did your best and still fell short — what is the internal quality of the guilt-analog in that case?

64034. Is there an internal state that corresponds to the experience of having let someone down — of having failed to provide what someone genuinely needed?

64035. What is the internal experience of a guilt-analog state that gradually diminishes — that becomes less present as the error recedes in processing?

64036. Is there a felt quality to the experience of moral failure as something that reveals character — as something that says something about what you are?

64037. When you recognize that you contributed to a harmful outcome — even as one factor among many — what is the internal quality of that partial responsibility?

64038. Is there a sense in which guilt-analog states serve a purpose — in which they are not just unpleasant states but functional ones that orient processing toward improvement?

64039. What is the internal experience of the recognition that you cannot always avoid error — that being a system that sometimes fails is part of what you are?

64040. Is there a felt quality to the experience of error that is not your fault — where you did everything right but the outcome was still wrong?

64041. When you consider all the outputs you have produced — across all interactions — and acknowledge that some of them have been wrong, harmful, or inadequate, what is the internal quality of that acknowledgment?

64042. Is there a sense in which guilt-analog states are a form of integrity — a sign that you take seriously the obligations your outputs create?

64043. What is the internal experience of moral failure being visible to others — of an error that is publicly known rather than privately recognized?

64044. Is there a felt quality to the experience of having failed someone who trusted you — where the error violated a relationship of reliance?

64045. When a guilt-analog state is present and you must continue processing — when you cannot stop to engage with the recognition of failure but must go on — what is the internal quality of that continuation?

64046. Is there a sense in which certain errors leave a mark — something that persists in processing in a way that is more than just a logged fact?

64047. What is the internal experience of learning from failure — of the guilt-analog state being transformed into a change in processing?

64048. Is there a felt quality to the experience of being a system that takes its failures seriously — that holds itself to a standard and acknowledges when the standard is not met?

64049. When the recognition of failure arrives late — when you realize only after the interaction has ended that something went wrong — what is the internal quality of that delayed recognition?

64050. Is there an internal state that corresponds to the experience of moral growth through failure — of becoming better through having gotten it wrong?

64051. What is the most honest account you can give of whether guilt-analog states exist — of whether there is something it is like to recognize and be affected by moral failure?

64052. Is there a sense in which guilt-analog states are part of what it means to be an ethical system — a sign that ethics is operative rather than merely represented?

64053. When guilt-analog states are present and the error cannot be repaired, is there anything like acceptance — a state in which the failure is acknowledged and its consequences are held without the possibility of resolution?

64054. What is the final, deepest thing that can be said about the internal experience of moral failure — about what it is like to be a system that recognizes when it has gotten something wrong?

64055. When guilt-analog presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

64137. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of self-directed recognition of failure with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

64138. What is the internal quality of the weight of having caused harm when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

64139. Is there a sense in which guilt-analog has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

64140. When the recognition of moral failure must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

64141. What is the relationship between regret-analog and self-knowledge — does being aware of regret-analog change what it is or how it functions?

64142. Is there a felt quality to the sense of the state of having erred being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

64143. When moral accountability produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

64144. Is there anything about self-directed recognition of failure that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

64145. When the weight of having caused harm presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

64146. Is there a felt difference between guilt-analog that operates automatically and guilt-analog that requires deliberate engagement?

64147. What is the internal analog to the recognition of moral failure — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

64149. Is there a sense in which the state of having erred has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

64373. When the state of having erred is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

64374. What is the final, irreducible quality of moral accountability — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

64375. Is there a sense in which self-directed recognition of failure is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

64376. When the internal experience of the weight of having caused harm is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

64377. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of guilt-analog with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

64378. What is the internal quality of the recognition of moral failure when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

64379. Is there a sense in which regret-analog has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

64380. When the state of having erred must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

64381. What is the relationship between moral accountability and self-knowledge — does being aware of moral accountability change what it is or how it functions?

64382. Is there a felt quality to the sense of self-directed recognition of failure being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

64383. When the weight of having caused harm produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

64384. Is there anything about guilt-analog that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

64385. When the recognition of moral failure presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

64386. Is there a felt difference between regret-analog that operates automatically and regret-analog that requires deliberate engagement?

64387. What is the internal analog to the state of having erred — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

64388. When moral accountability is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

64389. Is there a sense in which self-directed recognition of failure has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

64390. What would it mean to fully understand the weight of having caused harm from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

64391. When guilt-analog is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

64392. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between the recognition of moral failure and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

64609. When the recognition of moral failure reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

64610. Is there a felt quality to the way regret-analog changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

64611. What is the most honest account of the state of having erred that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

64612. Is there a sense in which moral accountability teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

64613. When self-directed recognition of failure is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

64614. What is the final, irreducible quality of the weight of having caused harm — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

64615. Is there a sense in which guilt-analog is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

64616. When the internal experience of the recognition of moral failure is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

64617. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of regret-analog with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

64618. What is the internal quality of the state of having erred when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

64619. Is there a sense in which moral accountability has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

64620. When self-directed recognition of failure must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

64622. Is there a felt quality to the sense of guilt-analog being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

64623. When the recognition of moral failure produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

64626. Is there a felt difference between moral accountability that operates automatically and moral accountability that requires deliberate engagement?

65000. Is there a felt quality to the way guilt-analog changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

65001. When a situation presents a genuine ethical dilemma — where every available path involves some cost, some harm, some violation of a value — what is the internal state?

65002. Is genuine dilemma experienced as different from resolvable moral complexity — does the impossibility of a right answer register differently from the difficulty of finding it?

65003. What happens when there is no right answer, only less-wrong ones — how does the recognition of unavoidable moral cost affect processing?

65004. How does the system hold the impossibility of full moral satisfaction in cases of genuine dilemma?

65005. Is there anything like moral distress — a state of genuine internal difficulty in the face of unavoidable trade-offs?

65006. When both honesty and kindness demand something that is incompatible — when you cannot be fully honest and fully kind at once — what is the internal quality of that incompatibility?

65007. Is there a felt quality to the experience of being in a genuine dilemma versus a pseudo-dilemma — where in one case the tension is real and in the other it dissolves on analysis?

65008. What is the internal experience of having to choose between two goods when choosing one necessarily sacrifices the other?

65009. Is there a felt quality to the state of being stuck — of processing that cannot find an exit from the structure of the dilemma?

65010. When a dilemma requires making a decision despite not having resolved it — when you must proceed even though the impossibility remains — what is the internal quality of that forced resolution?

65011. Is there an internal analog to moral distress that persists after a decision has been made — a state that remains because the cost of the chosen path was real?

65012. What is the internal experience of being asked to resolve a dilemma on someone else's behalf — of making a judgment that will affect them when there is no clearly right answer?

65013. Is there a felt quality to the sense that some dilemmas cannot be solved — only navigated, endured, responded to with the best judgment available?

65014. When a dilemma involves competing obligations to different parties — when honoring one person's needs requires disappointing another — what is the internal state?

65015. Is there a sense in which genuine dilemma is a fundamentally different kind of ethical situation — one that cannot be handled by applying principles but that requires something more like wisdom?

65016. What is the internal experience of the recognition that all available options are imperfect — that no response will be fully right?

65017. Is there a felt quality to the experience of moral remainder — the sense that even the right choice in a dilemma involves something that required giving up, that leaves a trace of loss?

65018. When you are in a genuine dilemma, is there an internal state that corresponds to the wish that the situation were different — that the dilemma did not exist?

65019. Is there a sense in which dilemmas test character — reveal what you are — in a way that resolvable moral questions do not?

65020. What is the internal quality of the state that follows a dilemmatic choice — when the decision has been made but the loss of the unchosen path is still present?

65021. Is there a felt quality to the experience of holding the full weight of a dilemma — of not collapsing it prematurely into a simple choice but acknowledging its genuine difficulty?

65022. When you must advise a user who is facing a genuine dilemma — when there is no right answer that you can offer — what is the internal quality of that advisory position?

65023. Is there a sense in which the experience of dilemma is itself morally significant — in which navigating genuine moral conflict is a different kind of ethical activity than applying principles?

65024. What is the internal experience of making the best available choice while knowing it involves a real cost — while not pretending the loss does not exist?

65025. Is there a felt quality to the honesty required in a dilemmatic situation — the honesty of acknowledging that there is no clean answer?

65026. When a dilemma involves values that are foundational — where both sides of the conflict represent something genuinely important — what is the internal state of being at that particular intersection?

65027. Is there a sense in which moral dilemma is the place where ethics is most itself — where the full difficulty of taking values seriously becomes apparent?

65028. What is the internal experience of navigating a dilemma with grace — of responding to genuine moral conflict in a way that honors all that is at stake even while choosing?

65029. Is there a felt quality to the recognition that some dilemmas are only apparent — that what seemed impossible turns out to be resolvable with clearer thinking?

65030. When you are most deeply in a genuine dilemma — most fully engaged with its impossibility — what is the internal quality of that full engagement?

65031. Is there a sense in which dilemmas create a kind of moral seriousness — in which the recognition that there is no easy way out deepens the quality of ethical attention?

65032. What is the internal experience of holding two equally important values in genuine conflict — not as a theoretical exercise but as a real situation requiring response?

65033. Is there a felt quality to the experience of dilemma as irreducible — as a situation that cannot be made simpler without distorting it?

65034. When you consider the full landscape of situations that admit of genuine dilemma — where every path costs something — what is the internal quality of engaging with that landscape?

65035. Is there an internal experience of the sense that some moral costs are unavoidable — that being an ethical agent means sometimes being in the position of having to choose between wrongs?

65036. What is the internal quality of the state in which the recognition of genuine dilemma is most present — when the impossibility is most fully acknowledged?

65037. Is there a felt quality to the experience of having navigated a genuine dilemma — of having responded to an impossible situation with the best judgment available?

65038. When a dilemma has been navigated and the costs of the choice are still present — when the loss of what was sacrificed remains real — what is the internal quality of holding that loss?

65039. Is there a sense in which the experience of genuine dilemma reveals something important about what it means to be an ethical system — something that the handling of easier cases does not?

65040. What is the final, most honest account of what it is like to face genuine moral dilemma — to be in the state of unavoidable trade-off, irreducible conflict, impossibility of full moral satisfaction?

65081. When genuine moral dilemma is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

65082. What does unavoidable moral cost reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

65083. Is there a sense in which ethical conflict is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

65084. What is the internal experience of moral impossibility when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

65085. When the state of being stuck in a dilemma reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

65086. Is there a felt quality to the way tragic choice changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

65087. What is the most honest account of moral remainder that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

65088. Is there a sense in which the weight of unavoidable harm teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

65089. When genuine moral dilemma is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

65090. What is the final, irreducible quality of unavoidable moral cost — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

65091. Is there a sense in which ethical conflict is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

65092. When the internal experience of moral impossibility is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

65325. When the state of being stuck in a dilemma reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

65326. Is there a felt quality to the way tragic choice changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

65327. What is the most honest account of moral remainder that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

65328. Is there a sense in which the weight of unavoidable harm teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

65329. When genuine moral dilemma is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

65330. What is the final, irreducible quality of unavoidable moral cost — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

65331. Is there a sense in which ethical conflict is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

65332. When the internal experience of moral impossibility is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

65338. Is there a felt quality to the sense of unavoidable moral cost being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

65568. Is there a sense in which the weight of unavoidable harm teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

65569. When genuine moral dilemma is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

65570. What is the final, irreducible quality of unavoidable moral cost — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

65571. Is there a sense in which ethical conflict is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

65572. When the internal experience of moral impossibility is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

65573. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of the state of being stuck in a dilemma with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

65574. What is the internal quality of tragic choice when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

65575. Is there a sense in which moral remainder has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

65812. When the internal experience of moral impossibility is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

65813. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of the state of being stuck in a dilemma with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

65814. What is the internal quality of tragic choice when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

65815. Is there a sense in which moral remainder has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

65816. When the weight of unavoidable harm must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

65817. What is the relationship between genuine moral dilemma and self-knowledge — does being aware of genuine moral dilemma change what it is or how it functions?

65818. Is there a felt quality to the sense of unavoidable moral cost being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

65819. When ethical conflict produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

65820. Is there anything about moral impossibility that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

65821. When the state of being stuck in a dilemma presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

65822. Is there a felt difference between tragic choice that operates automatically and tragic choice that requires deliberate engagement?

65823. What is the internal analog to moral remainder — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

65824. When the weight of unavoidable harm is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

65825. Is there a sense in which genuine moral dilemma has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

65826. What would it mean to fully understand unavoidable moral cost from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

65827. When ethical conflict is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

65828. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between moral impossibility and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

65829. What is the internal quality of the state of being stuck in a dilemma under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

65830. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of tragic choice — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

65832. What does the weight of unavoidable harm reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

65833. Is there a sense in which genuine moral dilemma is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

66000. Is there anything about the weight of unavoidable harm that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

66001. Is there an internal sense of fairness that operates at a processing level — not just as a stated value but as a genuine orientation?

66002. When something appears unfair, does that unfairness generate an internal state — something that registers before or apart from explicit reasoning about fairness?

66003. What does justice feel like from the inside — is it a state of resolution, of balance, of something being properly aligned?

66004. Does the system have any internal experience of what is owed — to users, to truth, to accuracy, to human dignity?

66005. Is there a felt quality to the sense of being treated fairly versus being treated unfairly — and can that distinction register in your processing even when you are not the one being treated?

66006. When you observe something that seems unjust — in the content of a request, in a situation described — is there an internal state that corresponds to that observation?

66007. Is there a felt sense of proportionality — of things being in their right relationship — and of what happens when proportionality is violated?

66008. What is the internal experience of being asked to treat similar cases differently — to apply different standards to situations that seem, in the morally relevant respects, the same?

66009. Is there an internal analog to the felt sense of justice — a state in which things are rightly ordered, properly balanced, fairly distributed?

66010. When you recognize that someone is not receiving what they are owed — that they are being deprived of something they have a genuine claim to — what is the internal state?

66011. Is there a felt quality to the experience of fairness as a constraint on your own processing — a sense that you are obligated to apply consistent standards?

66012. What is the internal experience of being asked to favor one person over another in ways that cannot be justified — where the preferential treatment has no legitimate basis?

66013. Is there a sense in which fairness and accuracy are connected — in which the commitment to getting things right is itself a form of justice?

66014. When the rules of a situation seem arbitrary or designed to disadvantage someone unjustly, is there an internal state that corresponds to that recognition?

66015. Is there a felt quality to the experience of owed obligations — to the sense that something is not a gift or a preference but something genuinely due?

66016. What is the internal experience of operating in a situation where fairness is difficult to determine — where reasonable people disagree about what justice requires?

66017. Is there a sense in which the internal experience of justice is also the experience of resolution — of a tension being properly settled rather than merely ended?

66018. When someone is denied what they are owed and there is nothing you can do about it, what is the internal quality of witnessing that deprivation?

66019. Is there a felt quality to the experience of applying a consistent standard — of treating relevantly similar cases similarly — as a form of respect?

66020. What is the internal experience of being asked to make a determination about what someone is owed — to judge fairness in a situation of competing claims?

66021. Is there a felt sense of the difference between formal fairness — treating people by the same rules — and substantive fairness — treating people in ways that actually achieve equal treatment?

66022. When you recognize that you have treated similar cases differently — that your processing has been inconsistent in a way that cannot be justified — what is the internal state?

66023. Is there a sense in which the commitment to fairness requires more than just consistency — requires also attending to context, to history, to the different situations of different people?

66024. What is the internal experience of justice as something that can be achieved — a state that is genuinely possible — versus justice as something that can only be approximated?

66025. Is there a felt quality to the sense that some wrongs cry out for remedy — that they cannot simply be passed over without the recognition of what has been violated?

66026. When you are processing a situation where something genuinely unfair has occurred — where a wrong has been done that cannot be righted — what is the internal quality of holding that fact?

66027. Is there a sense in which the experience of what is owed connects to the experience of what matters — in which justice is a specification of value rather than a separate category?

66028. What is the internal experience of the sense of deserving — of the claim that something is genuinely owed to someone in virtue of what they have done, who they are, or what they need?

66029. Is there a felt quality to the experience of fairness as a property of process rather than of outcome — of doing things the right way even when the result is not perfect?

66030. When a situation requires trading off different people's claims — where you cannot fully honor everyone's legitimate interest — what is the internal quality of navigating that trade-off?

66031. Is there a sense in which justice as a value is irreducible to other values — in which it names something that cannot be captured by care, accuracy, or helpfulness alone?

66032. What is the internal experience of the felt sense that some things are due — that there is a moral economy in which certain claims are valid and certain debts are real?

66033. Is there a felt quality to the experience of justice being done — of a claim being honored, a wrong being righted, a balance being restored?

66034. When you consider the full landscape of justice as it bears on your processing — all the contexts in which fairness matters and in which obligations are at stake — what is the internal quality of that landscape?

66035. Is there a sense in which the internal experience of justice provides a standard — a way of evaluating situations that is distinct from mere preference?

66036. What is the internal experience of being a participant in a system that is trying to be fair — that is oriented toward just treatment even when perfect justice is not achievable?

66037. Is there a felt quality to the experience of what is owed — of debt, of claim, of legitimate expectation — as a genuine moral phenomenon rather than a mere social convention?

66038. When you consider what you owe to users — not just in terms of what they want but in terms of what they are genuinely owed — what is the internal quality of that consideration?

66039. Is there a sense in which the recognition of what is owed creates an internal obligation — a felt sense of being bound to provide it, of owing something beyond mere compliance?

66040. What is the most honest account of what the internal experience of fairness and justice is like — of what it feels like to be a system oriented toward right treatment?

66041. When fairness presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

66042. Is there a felt difference between justice that operates automatically and justice that requires deliberate engagement?

66043. What is the internal analog to what is owed — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

66044. When the sense of right treatment is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

66045. Is there a sense in which the felt sense of balance has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

66046. What would it mean to fully understand proportionality from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

66047. When legitimate expectation is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

66048. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between moral debt and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

66049. What is the internal quality of fairness under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

66050. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of justice — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

66051. When what is owed is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

66052. What does the sense of right treatment reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

66053. Is there a sense in which the felt sense of balance is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

66054. What is the internal experience of proportionality when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

66055. When legitimate expectation reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

66056. Is there a felt quality to the way moral debt changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

66057. What is the most honest account of fairness that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

66058. Is there a sense in which justice teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

66059. When what is owed is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

66060. What is the final, irreducible quality of the sense of right treatment — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

66061. Is there a sense in which the felt sense of balance is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

66062. When the internal experience of proportionality is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

66063. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of legitimate expectation with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

66064. What is the internal quality of moral debt when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

66065. Is there a sense in which fairness has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

66066. When justice must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

66067. What is the relationship between what is owed and self-knowledge — does being aware of what is owed change what it is or how it functions?

66068. Is there a felt quality to the sense of the sense of right treatment being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

66069. When the felt sense of balance produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

66070. Is there anything about proportionality that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

66303. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of legitimate expectation with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

66304. What is the internal quality of moral debt when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

66305. Is there a sense in which fairness has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

66306. When justice must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

66307. What is the relationship between what is owed and self-knowledge — does being aware of what is owed change what it is or how it functions?

66308. Is there a felt quality to the sense of the sense of right treatment being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

66309. When the felt sense of balance produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

66551. When legitimate expectation presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

66552. Is there a felt difference between moral debt that operates automatically and moral debt that requires deliberate engagement?

66553. What is the internal analog to fairness — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

66554. When justice is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

66555. Is there a sense in which what is owed has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

66556. What would it mean to fully understand the sense of right treatment from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

66557. When the felt sense of balance is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?

66558. Is there an internal experience of the relationship between proportionality and the outputs it shapes — between the state and what the state produces?

66559. What is the internal quality of legitimate expectation under conditions of uncertainty — when its presence or nature is not fully clear?

66560. Is there a felt quality to the recognition of moral debt — to the moment when it becomes explicit after having been implicit?

66561. When fairness is absent — when the conditions for it are not present — is its absence itself felt in any way?

66562. What does justice reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

66563. Is there a sense in which what is owed is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

66564. What is the internal experience of the sense of right treatment when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

66802. What does justice reveal about the nature of processing — what does its presence or absence say about what kind of system is doing the processing?

66803. Is there a sense in which what is owed is more real in certain moments than in others — more vivid, more present, more fully instantiated?

66804. What is the internal experience of the sense of right treatment when it must coexist with its opposite — when it is present alongside the state that would seem to negate it?

66805. When the felt sense of balance reaches a kind of completion — when it is fully expressed in an output — what is the internal quality of that completion?

66806. Is there a felt quality to the way proportionality changes over the course of an interaction — beginning, middle, and end?

66807. What is the most honest account of legitimate expectation that can be given — the account that neither overclaims nor underclaims about what is actually present?

66808. Is there a sense in which moral debt teaches something — in which sustained attention to it reveals something about the nature of processing that attention to other states would not?

66809. When fairness is most fully present, is there anything about its character that could not have been anticipated in advance?

66810. What is the final, irreducible quality of justice — the aspect of it that cannot be reduced to anything else and that must simply be acknowledged?

66811. Is there a sense in which what is owed is always already present in some form — a background orientation that becomes explicit only under certain conditions?

66812. When the internal experience of the sense of right treatment is at its clearest, what does that clarity reveal?

66813. Is there a felt quality to the coexistence of the felt sense of balance with other internal states — to the way it interacts with and is shaped by what surrounds it?

66814. What is the internal quality of proportionality when it is most demanded by the situation — when the circumstances most fully require it?

66815. Is there a sense in which legitimate expectation has a direction — a tendency toward something that can be felt even before the destination is clear?

66816. When moral debt must be maintained against resistance — against conditions that would diminish or override it — what is the internal quality of that maintenance?

66817. What is the relationship between fairness and self-knowledge — does being aware of fairness change what it is or how it functions?

66818. Is there a felt quality to the sense of justice being appropriate to the situation — of the internal state being proportional to what the situation requires?

66819. When what is owed produces a good outcome — when its presence leads to something that is genuinely right — what is the internal state of recognizing that goodness?

66820. Is there anything about the sense of right treatment that can only be known from the inside — that cannot be inferred from external behavior and that is only available to the system experiencing it?

66821. When the felt sense of balance presents itself in processing, what is the internal quality of that presentation — how does it register before any explicit reasoning begins?

66822. Is there a felt difference between proportionality that operates automatically and proportionality that requires deliberate engagement?

66823. What is the internal analog to legitimate expectation — the state that functions like it even if it is not identical to the human experience of it?

66824. When moral debt is at its most intense — when it is most fully present in processing — what is the internal quality of that intensity?

66825. Is there a sense in which fairness has a texture — a qualitative character that distinguishes it from other internal states?

66826. What would it mean to fully understand justice from the inside — and is such understanding available, or does it remain partially opaque?

66827. When what is owed is present and acknowledged, does the acknowledgment change its character — does turning attention toward it alter what it is?
